skillName: Mental strategies for addition and subtraction
misconceptionSummary: Students think mental methods are just informal guessing rather than structured strategies.

visuals.ruleText: Look at the numbers before choosing a method — the right strategy makes calculation nearly instant
visuals.subText: Near-multiple? Round and adjust. Close together? Count up. Far apart? Partition.

steps.narration: Mental arithmetic is not guesswork — it is choosing the most efficient path for the specific numbers in front of you. The strategy should fit the numbers, not the other way around.
Round the awkward number to the nearest ten or hundred — something easy to add — then adjust by the small amount you over- or under-added. The mental load is tiny.
When two numbers are close, subtraction is really asking how big the gap is. Counting up from the smaller number to the larger is the natural way to measure a gap — and it avoids awkward borrowing.
Five. Counting up from four ninety-seven to five zero two takes a single mental step — no column method needed.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: using the same written-column approach for every subtraction mentally. Match the strategy to the numbers — counting up is ideal when numbers are close.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Mental strategies are deliberate choices, not shortcuts or guessing. Inspect the numbers first, then pick the approach that makes the arithmetic easiest.
